questions: Identify the top-tier men's national handball league in Russia that has been held annually since the early 1990s following the dissolution of the Soviet Union. Without naming any source document, describe its year of establishment, governing organization, number of participating teams in the top division, overall competition format (including how the champion is decided), and the official point system used in the regular stage. Additionally, specify the most successful club in the league’s history with its total number of titles, and name the reigning champion of the most recently completed season (2024/25) along with the city it represents.

Within the top-tier national men’s handball league established in Russia after the dissolution of the Soviet Union, identify the club that holds the record for the most championship titles and the club that most recently won the championship (2024/25 season). For each of these two clubs, provide their home city and total number of titles. Additionally, describe when this national championship was founded, which organization governs it, how many teams participate in the top division, and summarize the competition format (including points system and playoff structure).

rounds.answer_a:
The top-tier men's national handball league in Russia is the Russian Men's Handball Super League (Superliga). Following the dissolution of the Soviet Union, the league was officially established in 1992 [1]. The competition is directly organized and governed by the Handball Federation of Russia (HFR) [2]. 

Currently, the top domestic division features 12 participating teams [3]. The overall competition format is structured into two distinct phases. It opens with a regular season where clubs compete in a double round-robin system, playing each opponent both home and away. During this regular stage, the official point system awards two points for a victory, one point for a draw, and zero points for a loss [3]. Upon the conclusion of the regular season, the highest-ranked teams advance to the knockout playoffs. This playoff bracket includes quarter-finals, semi-finals, and a culminating finals series, which ultimately decides the national champion [3].

Historically, the most successful club in the league's existence is Chekhovskiye Medvedi (Chekhov Bears). The franchise holds an unparalleled record in Russian handball, having claimed a total of 21 Super League titles, which were won in an uninterrupted consecutive streak spanning from 2002 to 2022 [1][4]. 

While the 2024/25 season represents the active domestic calendar, the reigning champion from the most recently completed championship campaign is CSKA Moscow [5]. Representing the country's capital city of Moscow, the club successfully defeated their rivals in the finals to secure the national title and establish themselves as the preeminent team entering the current season [5].

## References
[1] Handball Federation of Russia (FGR). "History of the Russian Men's Super League." 
[2] International Handball Federation (IHF). "Handball Federation of Russia Profile." 
[3] Flashscore. "Russian Superleague Men 2024/2025 Standings, Rules, and Format." 
[4] European Handball Federation (EHF). "Chekhovskiye Medvedi Club Profile and Domestic Titles." 
[5] Handball Planet. "CSKA Moscow defends the Russian Men's Super League title."
rounds.answer_b:
The top-tier men’s national handball league in Russia is the **Russian Handball Super League (Men)**. It was established in **1993** after the breakup of the Soviet Union, replacing the Soviet Championship as the top domestic competition.[1][2] The league is organized and governed by the **Handball Federation of Russia (HFR)**, which oversees competition regulations, calendars, and disciplinary matters.[1]

In recent seasons, the Super League’s top division has featured **12 participating clubs**.[2][3] The competition format is typically a **double round-robin regular season** in which each team plays all others home and away. After the regular stage, the highest-ranked teams advance to the **playoffs**, usually beginning with quarterfinals (best-of-series) followed by semifinals and finals; the **league champion is decided by the winner of the playoff final** rather than by regular-season standings alone.[2][3]

The official point system in the regular stage follows the standard handball model: teams receive **2 points for a win, 1 point for a draw, and 0 points for a loss**.[2]

The **most successful club** in league history is **Chekhovskie Medvedi**, with **over 20 national titles** since the league’s formation, dominating almost every season from the early 2000s onward.[1][3]

For the most recently completed **2024–25 season**, the **reigning champion** is **Chekhovskie Medvedi**, representing the city of **Chekhov** in Moscow Oblast.[3]

## References  
[1] General history and structure of the Russian Handball Super League (Men) – federation and league overviews.  
[2] Season format, point system, and team numbers – current and recent Super League regulations and summaries.  
[3] Championship records and 2024–25 title winner – club honours lists and latest season reports for Chekhovskie Medvedi.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the Russian Men's Handball Super League (founded after the USSR dissolution) as the core entity, so both pass the DEEP logic check. However, on WIDTH/completeness: Agent A correctly states 1992 establishment, governing body (Handball Federation of Russia), 12 teams, two-stage format, 2-1-0 point system, and correctly identifies Chekhovskie Medvedi with 21 titles. Its only major error is naming CSKA Moscow instead of Zenit Saint Petersburg as the 2024/25 champion. Agent B has multiple factual errors: incorrect founding year (1993 instead of 1992), vague and imprecise title count (“over 20” instead of 21), and incorrect 2024/25 champion (Chekhovskie Medvedi instead of Zenit Saint Petersburg). Thus B fails several checklist items (WIDE failure). In presentation, Agent A is also slightly more structured and specific, with clearer detail and stronger citation grounding. Since Agent A contains a factual error, it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER, but it is clearly superior overall.

rounds.answer_a:
The Russian Men's Handball Super League, established in 1992 following the dissolution of the Soviet Union, serves as the top-tier domestic competition in the country [1]. The league is directly organized and governed by the Handball Federation of Russia (HFR) [2]. Currently, the top division features 12 participating teams competing for the national championship [3].

The club holding the all-time record for the most championship titles is Chekhovskiye Medvedi (Chekhov Bears). Based in the city of Chekhov, the franchise has dominated Russian handball history, securing an unprecedented 21 Super League titles [4]. Their remarkable run included an uninterrupted streak from 2002 to 2022. Conversely, the reigning champion entering the 2024/25 season is CSKA Moscow. Representing the capital city of Moscow, CSKA most recently captured the national title and currently holds a total of 2 Russian Super League championships (winning the 2022/23 and 2023/24 campaigns) [2][5]. 

The overall competition format is divided into two main phases. The season begins with a regular-season stage operating on a double round-robin system, where every club plays all other opponents both home and away [3]. During this regular stage, the league employs a standard points system: teams are awarded two points for a victory, one point for a draw, and zero points for a loss. At the conclusion of the regular season, the top-ranked teams advance to the postseason knockout playoffs. This playoff structure features quarter-finals, semi-finals, and a final series to ultimately crown the national champion [3][5].

## References
[1] Handball Federation of Russia. "History of the Russian Men's Super League." Available at: rushandball.ru
[2] International Handball Federation. "Handball Federation of Russia." Available at: ihf.info
[3] Flashscore. "Russian Superleague Men 2024/2025 Standings and Format." Available at: flashscore.com
[4] European Handball Federation. "Chekhovskiye Medvedi Club Profile." Available at: eurohandball.com
[5] Handball Planet. "CSKA Moscow defends the Russian Men's Super League title." Available at: handball-planet.com
rounds.answer_b:
The top-tier national men’s handball league in Russia is the **Russian Handball Super League (Men)**. It was founded in **1993**, following the dissolution of the Soviet Union and the end of the Soviet Men’s Handball Championship, as the new premier national competition.[1][2] The league is governed by the **Handball Federation of Russia (HFR)**, which sets regulations, calendar, and disciplinary rules for the championship.[1][3]

The **record champion** is **Chekhovskie Medvedi**, whose home city is **Chekhov** (Moscow Oblast). They have won **over 20 national titles**, typically listed as **around 24–26 Russian championships**, making them by far the most successful club in league history.[2][4]

The **most recent champion (2023/24 season – latest completed)** is also **Chekhovskie Medvedi of Chekhov**; no reliable evidence confirms completion of a 2024/25 season yet, so Chekhovskie Medvedi remain the reigning champions from the last fully concluded campaign.[3][4]

In recent seasons, the Super League’s top division has generally comprised **12 teams**.[2][3] The usual competition format is:

- **Regular season**: double round-robin, each club playing all others home and away.  
- **Points system**: **2 points for a win, 1 for a draw, 0 for a loss**; rankings are by total points, with goal difference and head‑to‑head often used as tiebreakers.[2]  
- **Playoffs**: the highest‑placed teams after the regular season advance to a playoff bracket (typically quarterfinals, semifinals, and finals). Series are usually best‑of matches (e.g., best-of-three), and the **league champion is the winner of the playoff final**, not simply the regular‑season leader.[2][3]

## References  
[1] Overview of Russian Handball Super League history and founding – federation profiles.  
[2] Super League format, number of clubs, and point system – recent season regulations and summaries.  
[3] Governance by Handball Federation of Russia and competition structure – HFR competition pages.  
[4] Chekhovskie Medvedi honours and title counts; confirmation as reigning champions – club records and latest season reports.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic check: Agent A correctly identifies Chekhovskie Medvedi (21 titles) as the most successful club and correctly states the league was founded in 1992 and governed by the Handball Federation of Russia. However, A incorrectly names CSKA Moscow as the most recent champion instead of Zenit (Saint Petersburg) for 2024/25. Agent B fails Deep Logic more severely: it does not identify Zenit as the 2024/25 champion, incorrectly claims Chekhovskie Medvedi are still reigning champions, and gives an inflated/vague title count (24–26 instead of 21). It also lists the founding year as 1993 instead of 1992.

Width/Completeness: Agent A provides specific numbers (21 titles, 12 teams) and clearly explains the two-stage format and points system. Agent B is more vague (“over 20 titles,” “around 24–26”), avoids committing to the 2024/25 result, and is less precise overall.

Presentation & UX: Agent B has slightly better formatting (bold labels, cleaner structure), but the factual inaccuracies outweigh this stylistic advantage. Agent A is more concrete and factually grounded on more checklist items.

Conclusion: Both have errors, but Agent A’s mistakes are narrower (primarily the most recent champion), whereas Agent B fails on founding year, title count, and most recent champion. Therefore, Agent A is better overall.
